name: Topic particle は
description: Marks the topic the rest of the sentence comments on. Written with は but pronounced wa.

example.text: 私は学生です。
example.translation: I am a student.

name: Subject particle が
description: Marks the grammatical subject, typically introducing new information or answering a who or what question.

example.text: 庭に猫がいます。
example.translation: There is a cat in the garden.

name: Object particle を
description: Marks the direct object of a verb.

example.text: 毎朝水を飲みます。
example.translation: I drink water every morning.

name: Particle に
description: Marks a point in time, a destination of movement, or the target of an action.

example.text: 七時に起きます。
example.translation: I get up at seven.

name: Particle で
description: Marks the place where an action happens or the means used to do it.

example.text: 図書館で勉強します。
example.translation: I study at the library.

name: Direction particle へ
description: Marks the direction of movement, emphasizing the direction rather than the arrival point. Written with へ but pronounced e.

example.text: 来月日本へ行きます。
example.translation: I am going to Japan next month.

name: Possessive particle の
description: Connects two nouns so the first one possesses or describes the second.

example.text: これは私の本です。
example.translation: This is my book.

name: Inclusive particle も
description: Replaces は or が to mean also or too.

example.text: 私も行きます。
example.translation: I will go too.

name: Particle と
description: Joins nouns in a complete list, or marks the person an action is done together with.

example.text: 友達と映画を見ました。
example.translation: I watched a movie with a friend.

name: Question particle か
description: Placed at the end of a sentence to turn it into a question, so no question mark is needed.

example.text: これは何ですか。
example.translation: What is this?

name: Copula です・だ
description: States that something is or equals something else. です is the polite form and だ the plain form.

example.text: 田中さんは先生です。
example.translation: Ms. Tanaka is a teacher.

name: Polite ます form
description: The polite non-past verb ending used with strangers, superiors, and in formal settings.

example.text: 毎日日本語を勉強します。
example.translation: I study Japanese every day.

name: Negatives ません・ない
description: Negates a verb. ません is the polite ending and ない the plain one.

example.text: 私は肉を食べません。
example.translation: I do not eat meat.

name: Past tense ました・た
description: Marks a completed action. ました is the polite ending and た the plain one.

example.text: 昨日映画を見ました。
example.translation: I watched a movie yesterday.

name: Te-form
description: A connective verb form used to chain actions in sequence and to build many further patterns such as requests and the progressive.

example.text: 朝ご飯を食べて、学校に行きます。
example.translation: I eat breakfast and then go to school.

name: Requests with てください
description: Attaches ください to the te-form to make a polite request.

example.text: ここに名前を書いてください。
example.translation: Please write your name here.

name: Progressive ている
description: The te-form plus いる describes an action in progress or a state that continues.

example.text: 今、雨が降っています。
example.translation: It is raining now.

name: い-adjectives
description: Adjectives ending in い that conjugate directly for tense and negation without the copula.

example.text: この本は面白いです。
example.translation: This book is interesting.

name: な-adjectives
description: Adjectives that take な before a noun and rely on the copula for tense and negation.

example.text: 静かな部屋が好きです。
example.translation: I like quiet rooms.

name: Adjective past and negative
description: い-adjectives change their own ending, as in 高かった and 高くない, while な-adjectives conjugate through the copula.

example.text: 昨日はとても暑かったです。
example.translation: It was very hot yesterday.

name: Existence with ある・いる
description: ある states that inanimate things exist and いる that people or animals do.

example.text: 机の上に本があります。
example.translation: There is a book on the desk.

name: Desire with たい
description: Attaches to the verb stem to say the speaker wants to do something, and then conjugates like an い-adjective.

example.text: 日本に行きたいです。
example.translation: I want to go to Japan.

name: Suggestions with ましょう
description: The polite volitional ending used to suggest doing something together.

example.text: 一緒に昼ご飯を食べましょう。
example.translation: Let's have lunch together.

name: Counters
description: Numbers combine with counter words chosen by what is being counted, such as つ for objects and 人 for people.

example.text: りんごを三つ買いました。
example.translation: I bought three apples.

name: Reason with から
description: Follows a clause to give a reason, meaning because or so.

example.text: 忙しいから、パーティーに行けません。
example.translation: I cannot go to the party because I am busy.

name: Potential form
description: Expresses ability or possibility. Ru-verbs take られる and u-verbs shift their final syllable to the e-row plus る.

example.text: 漢字が少し読めます。
example.translation: I can read a little kanji.

name: Volitional form
description: The plain let's form, よう or おう, often combined with と思う to state an intention.

example.text: 来年、日本に行こうと思います。
example.translation: I am thinking of going to Japan next year.

name: Permission with てもいい
description: The te-form plus もいい asks for or grants permission.

example.text: ここで写真を撮ってもいいですか。
example.translation: May I take pictures here?

name: Prohibition with てはいけない
description: The te-form plus はいけない forbids an action.

example.text: この部屋に入ってはいけません。
example.translation: You must not enter this room.

name: Experience with たことがある
description: The plain past form plus ことがある states that someone has had the experience of doing something.

example.text: 富士山に登ったことがあります。
example.translation: I have climbed Mt. Fuji.

name: Obligation with なければならない
description: Expresses that an action is necessary or required.

example.text: 明日までにレポートを出さなければなりません。
example.translation: I have to hand in the report by tomorrow.

name: Conditional と
description: Joins clauses so that the second follows naturally or automatically whenever the first happens.

example.text: このボタンを押すと、ドアが開きます。
example.translation: When you press this button, the door opens.

name: Conditional ば
description: A general if form that focuses on the condition itself.

example.text: 安ければ、買います。
example.translation: If it is cheap, I will buy it.

name: Conditional たら
description: Formed from the past tense plus ら and covering both if and when-after situations.

example.text: 家に帰ったら、電話します。
example.translation: I will call you when I get home.

name: Conditional なら
description: Takes up something just mentioned as the condition, meaning if that is the case.

example.text: 京都に行くなら、秋がいいですよ。
example.translation: If you are going to Kyoto, autumn is a good time.

name: Hearsay そうだ
description: After a plain-form clause, そうだ reports something the speaker heard from another source.

example.text: ニュースによると、明日は雪が降るそうです。
example.translation: According to the news, it will snow tomorrow.

name: Appearance そう
description: Attached to a verb or adjective stem, そう says that something looks or seems a certain way.

example.text: このケーキはおいしそうですね。
example.translation: This cake looks delicious.

name: Change with ようになる
description: Describes a gradual change in ability or habit.

example.text: 日本語が話せるようになりました。
example.translation: I have become able to speak Japanese.

name: Decisions with ことにする
description: The dictionary form plus ことにする states a decision the speaker makes, while ことになる reports one decided elsewhere.

example.text: 毎朝走ることにしました。
example.translation: I decided to run every morning.

name: Favors with てあげる・てくれる・てもらう
description: Benefactive auxiliaries after the te-form mark who does a favor for whom.

example.text: 友達が宿題を手伝ってくれました。
example.translation: A friend helped me with my homework.

name: Passive form
description: The られる or れる form describes an action from the receiver's point of view, often with a nuance of being affected.

example.text: 弟にケーキを食べられました。
example.translation: My cake was eaten by my little brother.

name: Causative form
description: The させる or せる form expresses making or letting someone do something.

example.text: 母は妹に野菜を食べさせました。
example.translation: My mother made my sister eat vegetables.

name: Probability でしょう
description: Softens a statement into a guess or prediction, meaning probably.

example.text: 明日は晴れるでしょう。
example.translation: It will probably be sunny tomorrow.

name: Possibility かもしれない
description: After a plain form, かもしれない means might or may.

example.text: 午後は雨が降るかもしれません。
example.translation: It might rain in the afternoon.

name: Expectation はず
description: States that something is logically expected to be the case.

example.text: 彼はもう家に着いたはずです。
example.translation: He should have arrived home by now.

name: Contrast のに
description: Joins clauses to mean even though, with a nuance of surprise or dissatisfaction.

example.text: たくさん勉強したのに、試験に落ちました。
example.translation: Even though I studied a lot, I failed the exam.

name: Listing reasons with し
description: Lists several reasons or qualities while implying there may be more.

example.text: この店は安いし、おいしいし、よく来ます。
example.translation: This place is cheap and tasty, so I come here often.

name: Representative actions たり〜たりする
description: The past form plus り lists example activities without implying order or completeness.

example.text: 週末は本を読んだり、音楽を聞いたりします。
example.translation: On weekends I do things like reading and listening to music.

name: Simultaneous actions ながら
description: A verb stem plus ながら shows one person doing two actions at the same time.

example.text: 音楽を聞きながら勉強します。
example.translation: I study while listening to music.

name: Completion and regret てしまう
description: The te-form plus しまう marks that something finished completely, often with regret or that it was unintended.

example.text: 電車に傘を忘れてしまいました。
example.translation: I ended up leaving my umbrella on the train.

name: Conjecture らしい
description: Marks a conclusion drawn from what the speaker heard or observed, or a typical quality of something.

example.text: 彼は会社を辞めるらしいです。
example.translation: Apparently he is quitting the company.

name: Resemblance みたい
description: A colloquial way to say that something looks like or seems like something else.

example.text: 彼は子供みたいに喜んでいました。
example.translation: He was as delighted as a child.

name: Conjecture ようだ
description: States an inference from the speaker's own observation, more formal than みたい.

example.text: 部屋には誰もいないようです。
example.translation: It seems that nobody is in the room.

name: Limitation ばかり
description: Means nothing but, or right after a past verb, that an action has only just happened.

example.text: さっき昼ご飯を食べたばかりです。
example.translation: I just ate lunch a moment ago.

name: Preparation ておく
description: The te-form plus おく means doing something in advance or leaving something in its current state.

example.text: 旅行の前に切符を買っておきます。
example.translation: I will buy the tickets in advance before the trip.

name: Attempt てみる
description: The te-form plus みる means trying something to see how it turns out.

example.text: 初めてこの料理を作ってみました。
example.translation: I tried making this dish for the first time.

name: Change over time てくる・ていく
description: てくる describes a change leading up to now, while ていく describes one continuing into the future.

example.text: 日本語が上手になってきました。
example.translation: My Japanese has been getting better.

name: Causative-passive
description: The させられる form expresses being made to do something against one's will.

example.text: 子供のとき、毎日ピアノを練習させられました。
example.translation: As a child I was made to practice the piano every day.

name: Honorific language 尊敬語
description: Raises the listener or a third person with special verbs such as いらっしゃる and patterns like お〜になる.

example.text: 先生はもういらっしゃいました。
example.translation: The teacher has already arrived.

name: Humble language 謙譲語
description: Lowers the speaker's own actions with special verbs such as 伺う and patterns like お〜する.

example.text: 明日、そちらに伺います。
example.translation: I will visit you tomorrow.

name: Nominalizers の and こと
description: Turn a verb clause into a noun phrase so it can take particles.

example.text: 走るのが好きです。
example.translation: I like running.

name: Naming という
description: Links a name or quoted content to a noun, meaning called or that says.

example.text: 「ひまわり」という店を知っていますか。
example.translation: Do you know a shop called Himawari?

name: Explanation わけ
description: Draws a logical conclusion, and with ではない denies an implication the listener might draw.

example.text: 高いわけではありませんが、安くもないです。
example.translation: It is not that it is expensive, but it is not cheap either.

name: Extent ほど
description: Expresses degree, including so much that, and upper-bound comparisons with a negative.

example.text: 今日は昨日ほど寒くないです。
example.translation: Today is not as cold as yesterday.

name: Extent くらい・ぐらい
description: Marks approximate amounts or a minimal or extreme degree.

example.text: 駅まで十分くらいかかります。
example.translation: It takes about ten minutes to the station.

name: Topic marker について
description: Marks what a statement, discussion, or piece of writing is about.

example.text: 日本の歴史について発表しました。
example.translation: I gave a presentation about Japanese history.

name: Means and variation によって
description: Marks the agent, means, or cause, and with 違う expresses variation from case to case.

example.text: 国によって習慣が違います。
example.translation: Customs differ from country to country.

name: Purpose and cause ために
description: The dictionary form plus ために expresses purpose, and a noun plus のために also benefit or cause.

example.text: 家族のために働いています。
example.translation: I work for the sake of my family.

name: Regret ばよかった
description: The ば-conditional plus よかった expresses regret about what one did or did not do.

example.text: もっと早く出発すればよかったです。
example.translation: I should have left earlier.

name: While うちに
description: Means doing something while a state still lasts, before it changes.

example.text: 温かいうちに食べてください。
example.translation: Please eat it while it is warm.

name: Certainty に違いない
description: States a confident conclusion, meaning it must be so.

example.text: 彼はまだ会社にいるに違いありません。
example.translation: He must still be at the office.

name: Social impossibility わけにはいかない
description: Says an action is unacceptable for social or moral reasons even if physically possible.

example.text: 大事な会議だから、休むわけにはいきません。
example.translation: It is an important meeting, so I cannot simply skip it.

name: Without doing ずに
description: A formal equivalent of ないで, meaning doing one thing without doing another.

example.text: 朝ご飯を食べずに家を出ました。
example.translation: I left home without eating breakfast.

name: Generalization ものだ
description: States general truths, fondly remembered habits of the past, or heartfelt exclamation.

example.text: 子供のころ、よく川で泳いだものです。
example.translation: When I was a child, I often used to swim in the river.

name: Advice ことだ
description: After a dictionary or ない form, gives firm advice about the thing to do.

example.text: 上手になりたいなら、毎日練習することです。
example.translation: If you want to improve, the thing to do is practice every day.

name: Left as is っぱなし
description: A verb stem plus っぱなし means something was left in a state, usually carelessly.

example.text: 窓を開けっぱなしにして寝てしまいました。
example.translation: I fell asleep leaving the window open.

name: Tendency がち
description: A verb stem or noun plus がち indicates an undesirable tendency.

example.text: 冬は風邪をひきがちです。
example.translation: In winter, people tend to catch colds.

name: Polite inability かねる
description: A verb stem plus かねる politely says the speaker is unable to do something.

example.text: その質問にはお答えしかねます。
example.translation: I am unable to answer that question.

name: Risk かねない
description: A verb stem plus かねない warns that something undesirable could well happen.

example.text: このままでは大きな事故になりかねません。
example.translation: At this rate, it could well turn into a serious accident.

name: Regarding に関して
description: A formal way to mark the subject matter under discussion, like について.

example.text: この件に関して質問があります。
example.translation: I have a question regarding this matter.

name: Relative to わりに
description: Notes that something differs from what its premise would lead you to expect.

example.text: この店は値段のわりにおいしいです。
example.translation: This restaurant is good for the price.

name: Bad outcome あげく
description: After long or repeated effort, marks that things ended badly in the end.

example.text: 何時間も迷ったあげく、何も買いませんでした。
example.translation: After agonizing for hours, I ended up buying nothing.

name: Contrast 一方で
description: Presents two sides of something or two things happening at once.

example.text: 都会は便利な一方で、生活費が高いです。
example.translation: Cities are convenient, but on the other hand the cost of living is high.

name: Not only ばかりか
description: States that something goes beyond the first item, meaning not only but also.

example.text: 彼は英語ばかりか中国語も話せます。
example.translation: He can speak not only English but also Chinese.

name: As long as 限り
description: Means as long as a state holds, or to the full extent of what the speaker knows.

example.text: 私が知っている限り、彼は正直な人です。
example.translation: As far as I know, he is an honest person.

name: In the middle of 最中に
description: Marks that something happened right in the middle of an ongoing action.

example.text: 会議の最中に電話が鳴りました。
example.translation: The phone rang right in the middle of the meeting.

name: Every time たびに
description: Means that whenever one thing happens, another follows.

example.text: この歌を聞くたびに、学生時代を思い出します。
example.translation: Every time I hear this song, I remember my school days.

name: Along with とともに
description: A formal expression for together with, or for two changes advancing at the same time.

example.text: 気温の上昇とともに、電力の使用量も増えます。
example.translation: As temperatures rise, electricity use increases as well.

name: Credit and blame おかげで・せいで
description: おかげで credits something for a good result, while せいで blames it for a bad one.

example.text: 先生のおかげで、試験に合格できました。
example.translation: Thanks to my teacher, I passed the exam.

name: Although ものの
description: A formal although, where the expected result did not follow.

example.text: 免許は取ったものの、ほとんど運転していません。
example.translation: Although I got my license, I hardly ever drive.

name: As soon as や否や
description: A literary pattern where the second action happens the instant the first does.

example.text: ベルが鳴るや否や、生徒たちは教室を飛び出した。
example.translation: The moment the bell rang, the students dashed out of the classroom.

name: The instant なり
description: The dictionary form plus なり marks an unexpected action that followed immediately.

example.text: 彼は家に帰るなり、部屋に閉じこもった。
example.translation: As soon as he got home, he shut himself in his room.

name: Utmost feeling 限りだ
description: After an emotive adjective, expresses that the speaker feels it to the utmost degree.

example.text: 皆さんにお会いできて、うれしい限りです。
example.translation: I could not be happier to meet you all.

name: Sorry end 始末だ
description: Concludes a chain of bad developments with the sorry state things ended in.

example.text: 彼は遅刻を繰り返し、ついには会議を忘れる始末だ。
example.translation: He kept being late, and in the end he even forgot the meeting.

name: Covered in まみれ
description: A noun plus まみれ means covered in something unpleasant such as mud, sweat, or dust.

example.text: 子供たちは泥まみれになって帰ってきた。
example.translation: The children came home covered in mud.

name: Nothing but ずくめ
description: A noun plus ずくめ means made up entirely of that thing, often good things or a color.

example.text: 今年はいいことずくめの一年だった。
example.translation: This year was filled with nothing but good things.

name: Unique to ならでは
description: Marks a quality that only that particular person or place could produce.

example.text: この味は京都ならではのものだ。
example.translation: This flavor is something you can only find in Kyoto.

name: Ignoring をよそに
description: Means proceeding without regard for others' worries or objections.

example.text: 親の心配をよそに、彼は留学を決めた。
example.translation: Ignoring his parents' worries, he decided to study abroad.

name: Only at the point に至って
description: Marks that only when an extreme stage was reached did something finally happen.

example.text: 締め切りの前日に至って、ようやく準備を始めた。
example.translation: Only on the day before the deadline did he finally start preparing.

name: Contrary to expectation と思いきや
description: The speaker expected one thing, but the opposite turned out to be true.

example.text: 合格したと思いきや、名前は名簿になかった。
example.translation: Just when I thought I had passed, my name was not on the list.

name: Missed chance そびれる
description: A verb stem plus そびれる means failing to do something one meant to do.

example.text: 言いそびれたことがあって、まだ気になっている。
example.translation: There is something I failed to say, and it still bothers me.

name: On the verge んばかりに
description: Describes a state so intense it looks about to spill over into action.

example.text: 彼女は泣き出さんばかりの顔でこちらを見た。
example.translation: She looked at me as if she were about to burst into tears.

name: Starting with を皮切りに
description: Marks the first event in a series that then continues one after another.

example.text: 東京公演を皮切りに、全国ツアーが始まる。
example.translation: Starting with the Tokyo performance, the nationwide tour begins.

name: Unbearable に堪えない
description: Means too painful to see or hear, or with emotion nouns, feeling something deeply.

example.text: その事故の映像は見るに堪えない。
example.translation: The footage of the accident is unbearable to watch.

name: Prohibition べからず
description: An archaic written prohibition seen on signs and in maxims.

example.text: 芝生に入るべからず。
example.translation: Keep off the grass.
